audit_status.source_base: Greek Acts text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for Macedonia/Greece, Eutychus, Troas bread-breaking, travel itinerary, Miletus farewell, serving with tears, Spirit-bound Jerusalem, guarding flock, no coveting, blessed to give.

verses.restored: After the uproar ceased, Paul sent for the disciples, encouraged them, said farewell, and departed to go to Macedonia.
Passing through those regions and encouraging them with many words, he came to Greece.
After spending three months, when a plot was made against him by the Judeans as he was about to sail to Syria, he determined to return through Macedonia.
Sopater of Berea, son of Pyrrhus, accompanied him; also Aristarchus and Secundus from Thessalonica, Gaius from Derbe, Timothy, and Tychicus and Trophimus from Asia.
These went ahead and were waiting for us in Troas.
But we sailed away from Philippi after the days of Unleavened Bread, and in five days came to them at Troas, where we spent seven days.
On the first day of the week, when we were gathered to break bread, Paul was reasoning with them, intending to depart the next day; he extended the word until midnight.
There were many lamps in the upper room where we were gathered.
A certain young man named Eutychus, sitting at the window, being overcome by deep sleep as Paul reasoned at length, was carried down by sleep and fell from the third story, and was taken up dead.
Paul went down, fell upon him, and embracing him said, Do not be troubled, for his life/soul is in him.
Going up, breaking the bread and eating, he conversed for a long while until daybreak, and so departed.
They brought the child alive and were comforted not moderately.
We went ahead to the ship and sailed to Assos, intending there to take Paul aboard, for so he had arranged, intending himself to go by land.
When he met us at Assos, taking him aboard, we came to Mitylene.
Sailing from there, the next day we arrived opposite Chios; the next day we touched at Samos, and the day after we came to Miletus.
For Paul had decided to sail past Ephesus, so that he might not spend time in Asia; for he was hurrying, if it were possible for him, to be in Jerusalem for the day of Pentecost.
From Miletus he sent to Ephesus and called the elders of the assembly.
When they came to him, he said to them, You yourselves know from the first day I set foot in Asia how I was with you the whole time,
serving the Lord with all humility and tears and trials that happened to me by the plots of the Judeans,
how I held back nothing of what was profitable, not failing to announce to you and teach you publicly and house by house,
testifying both to Judeans and Greeks of turning/realignment toward the Divine and trust toward our Lord Jesus.
And now, look, bound in the Spirit, I am going to Jerusalem, not knowing the things that will meet me there,
except that the holy Spirit testifies to me city by city, saying that bonds and pressures await me.

But I make my life/soul of no account to myself as precious, if only I may complete my course and the service that I received from the Lord Jesus: to bear witness to the good news of the grace of the Divine.
And now, look, I know that all of you among whom I went about proclaiming the reign will see my face no more.
Therefore I testify to you in this day that I am clean from the blood of all,
for I did not hold back from announcing to you the whole counsel of the Divine.
Pay attention to yourselves and to all the flock, in which the holy Spirit set you as overseers, to shepherd the assembly of the Divine, which he acquired through his own blood.
I know that after my departure, heavy wolves will enter among you, not sparing the flock.
And from among your own selves men will arise speaking twisted things, to draw away the disciples after them.
Therefore stay awake, remembering that for three years, night and day, I did not cease admonishing each one with tears.
And now I entrust you to the Divine and to the word of his grace, which is able to build and to give the inheritance among all those sanctified.
I coveted no one's silver or gold or clothing.
You yourselves know that these hands served my needs and those with me.
In all things I showed you that by laboring thus it is necessary to help the weak and to remember the words of the Lord Jesus, that he himself said, It is more blessed to give than to receive.
Saying these things, he knelt down and prayed with them all.
There was considerable weeping by all, and falling upon Paul's neck, they were kissing him,
especially grieving over the word he had spoken, that they were about to behold his face no more. They escorted him to the ship.
